metadata.title: What 50-Dollar Silicon Does to Who Controls AI

beats.narration_text: Yassou — this is Liam, in for Bear. Based on the essay 'Why $50 of Silicon Changes Who Controls AI' by Aditi Shinde, for NortheasternISE / Humanitarians AI. What changes when AI doesn't need a server? Three constraints make edge deployment not just cheaper but categorically different: privacy, latency, and institutional independence. When the microcontroller owns the inference, the terms of service are the laws of physics.

props.segment: What 50-Dollar Silicon Does to Who Controls AI
props.command: What changes when AI doesn't need a server?
props.runningText: reading edge AI access and control analysis…
output: $10 microcontroller: keyword detection in real time.
Cloud AI: a corporation between you and the result. Edge AI: doesn't.

beats.narration_text: Cloud AI topology: your device sends data to a data center owned by a corporation. The corporation's model processes it. The result comes back. The data is on their servers. The model is theirs. The infrastructure is theirs. You access it through their terms of service, which can change. Edge AI topology: the model is on your device. The data never leaves. The inference runs locally. The corporation is not in the loop unless you explicitly send data out. The power distribution is different — cloud centralizes compute and data; edge distributes both. A network of a hundred ESP32-CAM boards at ten dollars each doing wildlife monitoring sends nothing to a cloud. It runs on batteries in the forest.

manim.description: Side-by-side topology. Left: cloud — many devices connecting to single central server. Data flows up. Results flow down. Labels: 'corporation controls model, data, infrastructure'. Right: edge — many devices, each with compute. No central connection. Labels: 'model on device', 'data stays local', 'no corporation in the loop'.

beats.narration_text: Three properties of edge AI that are categorical, not just cheaper. Privacy: the data never leaves the device. Medical transcription that processes audio locally means patient data stays on the device — not on a HIPAA-regulated server you rent from someone else. Latency: network round-trip adds 500 milliseconds minimum. Any decision that must happen faster than the network cannot use the cloud — edge is not an optimization, it is the only architecture. Institutional independence: when the microcontroller owns the inference, the terms of service are the laws of physics. One hundred ESP32-CAM boards at ten dollars each — twenty are stolen, eighty identify poachers. No subscription. No terms.

animated_deck.description: Three pillars of edge AI. Root: 'Edge AI constraints'. Pillar 1: Privacy — data stays on device, no cloud HIPAA exposure. Pillar 2: Latency — 500ms network minimum, edge is the only option for fast decisions. Pillar 3: Independence — terms of service are physics; 100 ESP32-CAM boards, no subscription.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Three properties of edge AI that are categorical, not just cheaper. Privacy: the data never leaves the device. Medical transcription that processes audio locall

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. A ten-dollar microcontroller runs keyword detection in real time. A forty-dollar phone transcribes speech offline. Cloud AI requires a corporation between you and the result. Edge AI does not. When the microcontroller owns the inference, the terms of service are the laws of physics. One hundred ESP32-CAM boards at ten dollars each — twenty stolen, eighty identifying poachers — no subscription, no terms of service, no data leaving the forest.

props.artifactTitle: What 50-Dollar Silicon Does to Who Controls AI
artifactLines: $10 microcontroller: keyword detection in real time. $40 phone: offline speech transcription.
Cloud AI: corporation between you and result. Edge AI: doesn't.
Terms of service on edge = laws of physics.
100 ESP32-CAM boards, 80 working, no subscription — wildlife monitoring without the cloud.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Here is the prompt — read it with me. 'Deploy a keyword-detection model on a microcontroller or single-board computer. What is the maximum model size before latency exceeds 100 milliseconds? What does the power draw look like at continuous inference?' The 100 millisecond threshold is real — it is the latency above which human perception notices delay. Find the model size that sits under it and measure what the continuous power draw costs. That number tells you the battery life for any battery-powered deployment. Liam, in for Bear.
